A detailed investigation was made of the phase diagram of silver-zirconium, particularly in the region 0 to 36 at. $\%$ Ag.

It was found that impurities in the range $0.05\%$ from the iodide-type zirconium were sufficient to introduce deviations from binary behavior; with partial removal of these impurities an increase in the $\alpha$ -phase solid solubility limit from 0.1 to 1.1 at. $\%$ Ag was observed.
Noyce, "The Equilibrium Diagram of Indium-Zirconium in the Region 0-26 at. pct In," Trans.
The temperature of the allotropic transition was found to rise with indium, terminating in a peritectoid reaction, $\beta (9.3\% \ln) + \gamma (22.4\% \ln) = \alpha (10.1\% \ln)$ at $1003^{\circ}C$ .
O'Brien, "Zirconium Equilibrium Diagrams," p 443-83 in The Metallurgy of Zirconium, ed. by B.
Kerze, Jr., McGraw-Hill Book Co., New York, 1955.

Complete miscibility of metal and salt exists in the KX-K systems, $X = F$ , Cl, Br, and I, at and above 904, 790, 727, and $717^{\circ}C$ , that is, as close as $+47$ , $+18$ , $-7$ , and $+22^{\circ}$ , respectively, to the melting points of the salts.

The asymmetry of the liquid-liquid coexistence areas, especially of the fluoride and chloride systems, as indicated by the consolidate compositions (20, 39, 44, and 50 mole % metal, respectively), is largely explained by the difference in molar volumes of salt and metal.

Miscibility in all proportions of cesium metal with cesium halides at and above the melting points of the pure salts, and of potassium metal with potassium fluoride $50^{\circ}$ above the melting point of the salt, occurs.

In 0.126 M $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ the solubility of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{WO}_4$ first shows a positive temperature coefficient of solubility and then, above $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , a negative coefficient of solubility.

In 1.26 M $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ the temperature coefficient of solubility is positive up to $285^{\circ}$ , the temperature at which two liquid phases form - a characteristic of $\mathrm{UO}_2\mathrm{SO}_4 - \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ solutions.

Data at low temperature up to point $F$ were obtained by direct analysis of equilibrated solutions, whereas the high-temperature data were obtained by the visual-synthetic method described in Sec 5.8.
